1: "His foundation is on the holy mountains." The Zion psalm opens by grounding the city of God in the divine choice of the holy mountains as foundation. Hebrews 12:22 ("you have come to Mount Zion, to the city of the living God, the heavenly Jerusalem") is the NT's reinterpretation: believers in Christ have "come to" the true Zion that Psalm 87 describes — not by geography but by covenant. The foundation of the city in the holy mountains is the security that Christ provides for his church: Matthew 16:18 ("on this rock I will build my church, and the gates of death will not overcome it").
2: "The LORD loves the gates of Zion more than all the dwelling places of Jacob." The divine preference for Zion over all other Israelite dwelling places is the OT form of the NT claim that Christ is the singular locus of divine presence. John 2:19-21 ("destroy this temple and I will raise it again in three days" — speaking of his body) transfers the temple/Zion presence-locus to Christ himself. Ephesians 2:20-22 (believers "built on the foundation of the apostles and prophets, with Christ Jesus himself as the chief cornerstone... a holy temple in the Lord") is the community form of this Zion-love.
3: "Glorious things are spoken of you, O city of God." The glorious things spoken of Zion are the eschatological promises of restoration, Gentile inclusion, and divine dwelling. Revelation 21:9-27 devotes sustained attention to the "glorious things" of the new Jerusalem — its radiance, its gates, its nations, its Lamb-light. The connection between Ps 87 and Rev 21 is direct: both describe an international city whose population comes from the nations, whose foundation is divine, and whose glory surpasses anything human. Christ is the Lamb at the center of that city (Revelation 21:23; Psalms 22:1).
4: "Among those who acknowledge me I will name Rahab and Babylon; and behold — Philistia, Tyre, and Ethiopia: 'This one was born there.'" The extraordinary claim: Israel's historic enemies (Rahab = Egypt; Babylon; Philistia; Tyre; Cush/Ethiopia) are enrolled in the divine register as born in Zion — citizens of the city of God. This is the OT anticipation of the Gentile inclusion that Paul elaborates in Romans 9-11 and Ephesians 2-3. Galatians 4:26 ("the Jerusalem that is above is free, and she is our mother") and Acts 2:5-11 (the nations gathered at Pentecost) are the NT fulfillments. In Christ, all nations find their true birthplace.
5: "Of Zion it will be said, 'This one and that one were born in her,' and the Most High himself will establish her." The divine establishing of Zion — not through human means but by the Most High himself — is the pattern of the church's founding. Matthew 16:18 ("I will build my church") is Christ's claim to be the one who establishes the true Zion-community. The "this one and that one" born in her — individuals from across the nations registered as Zion-born — is the phenomenon of Pentecost (Acts 2:41: "about three thousand were added") and the ongoing growth of the church from every tribe and tongue (Revelation 7:9).
6: "When the LORD records the enrollment of the peoples, he will write, 'This one was born there.'" The divine enrollment of peoples in the Zion-register is the OT form of the NT's "book of life." Luke 10:20 (Christ to the disciples: "rejoice that your names are written in heaven") and Revelation 21:27 ("only those whose names are written in the Lamb's book of life" will enter the new Jerusalem) are the fulfillments. The enrollment is now specifically in the book of the Lamb — the crucified and risen Christ whose death is the basis for the registration. Being born in Zion means being born again through him (John 3:3-5).
7: "Singers and dancers alike say, 'All my springs of life are in you.'" The closing image of the psalm: the community of worship, with singers and dancers, declaring that all their living-springs are in Zion. John 4:14 ("whoever drinks the water I give them will never thirst; indeed, the water I give them will become in them a spring of water welling up to eternal life") and John 7:37-38 ("rivers of living water will flow from within them") are the specific NT fulfillments. Christ is the living spring that Psalm 87 locates in Zion; in him, the city of God becomes available to all who drink. Revelation 22:1 (the river of the water of life flowing from the throne of God and the Lamb) is the eschatological form.
